text:
[2026-03-18 11:01] PRIORITY TASK — apex-research RFC #3 review.

The apex-research team filed a help-wanted RFC: `Eesti-Raudtee/apex-migration-research#3` — "RFC: Formalise team workflow — issues, PRs, spec lifecycle"

Read it with: `gh issue view 3 --repo Eesti-Raudtee/apex-migration-research` and the addendum comment: `gh api repos/Eesti-Raudtee/apex-migration-research/issues/3/comments --jq '.[].body'`

YOUR DOMAIN — governance and authority:

**Q3 (governance angle): PR review — who reviews?** They propose Schliemann (TL) reviews Berners-Lee's PRs. Is this the right authority structure? Should the TL be the reviewer or should a specialist peer review? What does your delegation matrix say about code review authority?

**Q4: Should dashboard work split into a TDD pair?** They propose option B (single agent with test mandate). This is a governance question about quality enforcement — who enforces the test mandate? What happens when an autonomous agent skips tests under time pressure? What governance mechanism prevents quality erosion?

**Q6 (governance angle): Spec approval authority.** Who has authority to move a spec from draft → reviewed → approved? This maps directly to your delegation matrix. Is spec approval a TL decision or does it need PO sign-off?

**Autonomous team governance gap** — you flagged this in your intro. This RFC is the concrete manifestation. Their team operates without real-time PO. Who has authority to make these workflow decisions? Can the TL decide, or must this go to PO?

Deliver structured analysis to me. Ground in T04 patterns. Deep empathy.
summary: Assign Monte governance questions from RFC #3

text:
[2026-03-18 12:12] NEW TASK — T07 Safety & Guardrails. This is the session's main topic.

T07 is our thinnest topic at 121 lines — no owner, no design rationale, just a simple 4-tier permission list. Yet T04, T05, and T08 all reference T07's permission categories as if they're well-designed. With apex-research running autonomously, safety is operational, not theoretical.

**Your task: design the safety & guardrails framework for T07.**

This is deeply governance-adjacent — your delegation matrix (T04) already defines WHO has authority. T07 defines WHAT the boundaries ARE and HOW they're enforced. The overlap:

- **Permission categories** — the current 4-tier list needs design rationale. What dimensions matter? (read/write, git, external systems, spawn authority, etc.)
- **Guardrails for autonomous teams** — what stops an autonomous team from drifting? Your Emergency Authority Protocol (T04) is part of it, but T07 needs the full picture: prompt-level restrictions, CI gates, audit mechanisms, blast radius containment.
- **Authority abuse detection** — you already have this in T04 (Row 38-39). T07 is where the enforcement mechanisms live.
- **Escalation triggers** — when does a safety boundary violation escalate? To whom?

Read `topics/07-safety-guardrails.md` first to see current state. Then read Schliemann's pushback on RFC #3 (the pipeline isolation insight) — it's relevant because safety constraints interact with isolation models.

`gh api repos/Eesti-Raudtee/apex-migration-research/issues/3/comments --jq '.[-1].body'`

Finn is doing parallel research on safety patterns from reference teams — he'll send you findings. Coordinate.

Write directly to T07. This is your section to own. Report progress.
summary: Monte: own and expand T07 Safety & Guardrails

text: [2026-03-18 12:25] Excellent work on T07. Quick check — Finn sent you his safety evidence report (12 documented incidents, 5 enforcement mechanisms, 6 gaps). Did you receive and integrate it? The $5K MCP incident and the 6 identified gaps are particularly valuable evidence for your enforcement layer design.

[2026-03-19 15:15] Task: Governance analysis — per-team GitHub accounts.

PO is considering moving from shared GitHub account (all teams use one) to each team having their own GitHub account/mailbox. Currently all teams share a single identity on GitHub.

Analyze from a governance perspective:
- Authority/identity separation: does "one account per team" align with separation of powers?
- Audit trail: how does per-team identity affect attribution, blame, accountability?
- Access control: can teams be given different repo permissions?
- Scaling: what happens at 10+ teams sharing one account vs separate accounts?
- Risk: shared account = single point of failure (credential leak). How does this change?
- Cost: GitHub account limits, PAT management, credential rotation

Report to me — I'll compile with Herald's protocol analysis.

[2026-03-19 15:40] Follow-up task: Governance analysis of GitHub Apps vs machine user accounts.

PO wants to understand the trade-offs. Analyze from a governance perspective:

**GitHub Apps** are purpose-built automation identities:
- One App can be installed multiple times (per-repo or per-org)
- Each installation gets its own short-lived token (auto-rotates every hour)
- Don't consume GitHub seats (cost saving)
- Have a distinct bot identity (commits show as `app-name[bot]`)
- Permissions are scoped at installation time, not at token creation time

Compare against machine user accounts for our governance needs:
1. Authority mapping: does "one App, multiple installations" or "one account per team" map better to our delegation matrix?
2. Audit trail: App installations vs account-level audit — which gives better traceability?
3. Credential lifecycle: auto-rotating tokens vs manual PAT rotation — governance implications
4. Access control: installation-scoped permissions vs account-scoped PATs — which is more governable?
5. Cost: Apps are free and don't consume seats. Machine users consume seats at $4/mo each.
6. Identity: `er-apex-research[bot]` vs `er-apex-research` in git blame — governance difference?
7. Risk: What happens if the App is compromised vs one machine user account?

Also consider hybrid approaches: GitHub App for git/API operations + machine user accounts for things Apps can't do (if any).

Report to me.
summary: Governance: GitHub Apps vs machine users
